Author: Ahmed Elshayeb
Title: The Half-Life of Mastery
Text:
The Half-Life of Mastery
From YOLO Mode to Orchestrator — and What Comes After
A few weeks ago I had six terminals open — four running Claude Code agents, two for manual oversight — all working on the same feature in Quanto, the open-source quantitative research platform I’ve been building for the past year. The feature was conflict resolution in the ingestion pipeline: handling data that arrives from multiple vendors with contradictory values, misaligned timestamps, schemas that almost match but don’t. The canonicalization module. Not glamorous work, but the kind of structural plumbing that either works silently or ruins everything downstream.
I’d gotten here fast. In three months I’d gone from a single YOLO agent to four parallel CLI instances running against the same codebase. I’d built a custom orchestration workflow: a set of shared documents — PRD, ARCHITECT, FEATURE_SET — that each agent reads on startup, combined with Git history so it can figure out where to pick up. It was held together with discipline and convention, not infrastructure. But it worked. For a while.
Here’s what actually happens when you run four agents on a shared codebase with a document-based coordination system:
The agents start strong. Clear, focused, following the spec. But as the context window fills — as the conversation accumulates tool calls, file reads, error traces, corrections — they get lazy.
Not dramatically wrong. Lazy.
They start cutting corners on the architecture doc. They hardcode a value the spec explicitly marks as configurable. They implement something that already exists three directories over because the context window is too bloated for them to find it. And the longer the task runs, the worse it gets. An agent that was sharp at message 10 is unreliable by message 80.
And when something crashes mid-pipeline — which, in a multi-step ingestion workflow that runs for an hour, it does — I can’t resume. I have to restart from the beginning, eating the cost of everything that already ran.
I closed my laptop and went for a walk. Not because I was frustrated with the agents. The agents were fine — individually, each one was doing reasonable work. I was frustrated because I had accidentally promoted myself to the coordination layer — and I was not designed for that job. I wasn’t building the canonicalization module anymore. I was babysitting a workflow. On a typical session, roughly 30% of my time went to coordination — checking agent status, reconciling conflicts, restarting drifted sessions — instead of the actual engineering work I’d sat down to do.
This is the moment where most people would say: AI tools are evolving fast, you just need to keep up. And if this were just about tools, they’d be right. Tools have always churned. New frameworks, new editors, new CI pipelines — the JavaScript ecosystem alone has been rotating build tools for a decade. That’s tool churn. We’ve been living with that for years.
What’s different now is paradigm compression: the developer–code relationship is changing faster than humans can internalize it. Not the tools. The relationship.
Writing code → reviewing diffs → delegating tasks → orchestrating fleets.
Each of these is a structurally different way of working, and the transitions are happening in months. I went from writing code by hand to orchestrating four agents in three months. By the time I’d built real fluency in one mode, the frontier had already moved to the next.
I’ve spent seven years building ML systems for quantitative trading. In that world, I learned to recognize regime shifts — the moments where a strategy stops working not because the model is bad, but because the market’s underlying structure has changed. The same instinct applies here. The developers who will thrive aren’t the ones who learn the new tool first. They’re the ones who notice when the current paradigm is structurally breaking — and can name the assumption that broke.
Steve Yegge published a framework recently that maps this progression better than anything else I’ve seen. He calls it the evolution of the AI-assisted developer, and it describes eight stages from zero AI usage to building your own multi-agent orchestrator. I want to walk through those stages — not as Yegge describes them, but as I experienced them. Because the map is only useful if you can locate yourself on it.
The 8 Stages of AI-Assisted Development
I’m borrowing Yegge’s framework from his “Welcome to Gas Town” post, but labeling each stage by the behavioral shift I went through — not the tool I was using. Tools are interchangeable. Behaviors aren’t.
Stage 1: The Passenger Seat
You install Copilot or add an AI extension to your IDE. You type a function signature and wait for the ghost text. Sometimes it’s good. Sometimes it autocompletes a for-loop you were about to write anyway and you think: okay, that saved me four seconds.
You highlight a block of code and ask the sidebar: “What does this do?” It gives you a reasonable explanation. You ask it to write a docstring. It writes a docstring. You correct two things in it and move on.
This is the stage where AI is a convenience, not a collaborator. You’re still driving. The AI is a passenger suggesting turns. Occasionally useful, occasionally wrong, never in control. Most of the industry was here in 2023. A lot of it still is.
The thing about Stage 1 is that it’s comfortable. Nothing changes about how you work. Your mental model of “developer writes code” is fully intact. The AI just makes some of the typing faster. You don’t have to trust it with anything that matters, because you’re not giving it anything that matters.
Stage 2: Permissions On
You start using a proper coding agent — something that can edit files, run shell commands, create new directories. But it asks permission for everything. “I’d like to modify auth.py
— approve?" "I'd like to run npm test
— approve?"
You click approve. You click approve again. You read the diff, decide it’s fine, click approve. You watch it run tests, read the output, decide the fix is correct, click approve.
This is the stage where you technically have an agent, but you’ve effectively turned it into the world’s most verbose pair programmer. Every action goes through you. The latency is terrible — not the model’s latency, your latency. You’re the bottleneck in a loop that could run in seconds if you weren’t in it.
But you’re learning something important at this stage, even if you don’t realize it: you’re building a mental model of what the agent gets right and what it gets wrong. You’re calibrating trust. That calibration is the prerequisite for everything that follows.
Stage 3: YOLO Mode
One afternoon you’re clicking “approve” for the fifteenth time on a change you haven’t even read, and you realize: I’m not actually reviewing these anymore. I’m just clicking a button. So you turn off permissions. All of them. The agent can edit files, run commands, install packages, delete things — without asking.
The first time it deletes a file and you don’t flinch, you’ve arrived.
Trust goes up. Anxiety goes down. And something shifts that you didn’t expect: your productivity doesn’t just increase incrementally — it jumps. Not because the agent got better. It didn’t. You got out of the way. The approval loop was costing more than you thought, not just in time but in cognitive overhead. Every “approve” click was a context switch that pulled you out of the problem and into the mechanism.
YOLO mode is where most developers experience their first real taste of AI-augmented speed. It’s also where a lot of them stop — because it feels like the ceiling. You have an agent. It’s fast. It mostly works. What more could there be?
Stage 4: The Agent Fills the Screen
Gradually, the code the agent writes starts to matter less as code and more as output. You stop reading it line by line. You start reading diffs. You’re reviewing, not writing.
The IDE begins to feel different. It’s less of an editor and more of a review interface. The agent proposes changes and you evaluate whether the change moved the system in the right direction. You care about behavior, not syntax. You care about whether the test passes, not how the function was implemented.
This is a subtle shift, but it changes your relationship to the codebase. You start thinking at a higher level of abstraction — not “how should this function handle the edge case” but “does this module do what I need it to do.” The agent handles the how. You own the what and the whether.
At this stage, some developers start feeling uneasy. There’s a specific anxiety that comes from not having written the code yourself — a sense that you should understand every line, that skimming diffs is somehow cheating. I felt it too. It passes. Not because you stop caring about code quality, but because you develop a different kind of quality signal: you’re looking for intent alignment, not style.
If you’re at this stage right now, you’re ahead of most of the industry. You’re also one behavioral shift away from a phase transition that will change how you think about development entirely.
Stage 5: The IDE Disappears
You close the IDE. Not dramatically — you just stop opening it. The terminal is the interface now. You type what you want, the agent builds it, diffs scroll by. You may or may not read them.
This is the stage where something important happens to your workflow: you stop thinking about code as something you write and start thinking about it as something you specify. The input isn’t keystrokes in an editor — it’s a description of intent in a terminal. The output isn’t a file you authored — it’s a system state you directed.
For me, the shift to Stage 5 happened on Quanto. I was building the experiment management layer — the part of the system that tracks which data snapshots, feature sets, and model configurations make up each experiment. It’s fiddly, spec-heavy work with lots of edge cases. I found myself writing multi-paragraph prompts in the terminal describing exactly what I wanted, then watching the agent build it. I could write a 300-word prompt faster than I could scaffold the files by hand. The agent was writing more code per day than I ever had. And the code was fine. Not brilliant — fine. Consistent, spec-compliant, testable.
The unease from Stage 4 is gone by now. You’ve accepted that your job isn’t to write code. Your job is to know what needs to be built, describe it precisely, and verify that what came back matches what you asked for. That’s not less valuable than writing code. It’s a different skill entirely.
Stage 6: The Multiplier
You open a second terminal. Then a third. Then a fourth.
It starts innocuously. The first agent is blocked — waiting on a test suite, running a long build, stuck on a dependency issue. You don’t want to wait. So you open another terminal, point a second agent at a different part of the codebase, and keep moving. That idle time disappears. You’re now running parallel.
At three to five agents, something clicks. The throughput is genuinely multiplied. One agent writes the API layer while another builds the data models while a third writes tests. You tab between terminals, check progress, course-correct, move on. You feel fast — faster than you’ve ever been — because you are fast. The parallelism is real and the results are real.
This is where most developers at the frontier are today. It’s productive, it’s exciting, and it works — as long as you can hold the full state of every agent in your head. At three agents, you can. At five, you’re stretching. The coordination is all happening in your working memory: what each agent is doing, what it finished, what it’s about to touch, which files are shared. There’s no system keeping track. You are the system.
Stage 6 feels like the final form of AI-assisted development. It isn’t.
Stage 7: The Wall
You push to ten. Maybe twelve. You have the tasks, you have the codebase surface area, you have the ambition. So you spin up more agents.
And it breaks. Not spectacularly — quietly.
At ten-plus agents, you can’t answer “what’s done?” without polling every terminal window. You switch to tab eight and discover the agent has been idle for fifteen minutes — finished its task, waiting for you, burning credits. You switch to tab three and find it’s gone off-spec, implementing something you already told tab six to handle. You switch to tab eleven and can’t remember what you assigned it.
The failure mode isn’t that the agents got worse. It’s that you can’t coordinate at this scale. Your working memory holds maybe five parallel threads. Beyond that, you’re not orchestrating — you’re sampling. You check whatever tab you remember to check, and the rest drifts.
This is the coordination wall. I hit it on Quanto, and I’ll describe exactly how it broke in the next section. But the important thing is what the wall means: it’s a structural ceiling on human-as-coordination-layer. No amount of terminal management fixes it, because the bottleneck isn’t your discipline — it’s your architecture.
Stage 8: The Orchestrator
Stage 8 is where you stop being the coordination layer and start building one.
The behavioral shift is total. You’re no longer managing agents — you’re managing work. You don’t care which agent is running in which terminal. You care whether the task is done, whether it passed verification, and whether it conflicts with other tasks in flight.
Most developers haven’t been here. Most don’t need to be — yet. I spent weeks here, building the document-based orchestration system I described in the opening, before I understood that the approach had a structural ceiling of its own. The question isn’t whether you need Stage 8 today. The question is whether you can recognize the moment when Stage 6 or 7 stops working — not because you’re doing it wrong, but because the paradigm can’t carry what you’re trying to build.
I’ll come back to what Stage 8 looks like with real infrastructure — and what it doesn’t solve — later. First, I want to stay with the wall.
The Coordination Wall
Forty-seven minutes into a pipeline run, the agent hit a type error in the canonicalization module. Not a hard bug — a schema mismatch a fresh agent would have caught if it were still anchored to the spec. But this agent wasn’t fresh. It was eighty messages deep, its context window bloated with tool calls and error traces, and it had drifted far enough from the architecture doc to introduce the inconsistency in the first place.
I had two options: debug the error and try to patch the agent’s context back into alignment, or restart the pipeline from scratch. I’d been here before. Debugging a drifted agent is like arguing with someone who’s forgotten the first half of the conversation — you can correct the immediate error, but the underlying context rot means the next mistake is already forming. So I restarted. Forty-seven minutes of compute, gone. By the time I’d re-oriented the agent, re-ingested the spec, and gotten the pipeline back to where it had been, I’d lost nearly two hours.
This is the failure that made me understand the coordination wall — not as an annoyance, but as an architectural boundary. Because the restart problem isn’t just about wasted time. It exposes a structural catch-22 at the heart of document-based multi-agent coordination.
Here’s the catch-22: when a pipeline crashes, “resume from where it stopped” is a different problem every time. The failure might be a flaky test, a dependency timeout, a type error introduced by another agent’s concurrent commit. Each scenario requires a recovery path — files to revert, state to reconstruct, prompts to re-orient the agent. You can specify all of this upfront. You can write the exhaustive system prompt that covers every recovery scenario, every edge case, every failure mode. I tried. But the more comprehensive your startup instructions, the more of the context window they consume. The agent spends its first several minutes just processing the instructions — reading the PRD, parsing the architecture doc, ingesting the feature spec, understanding the recovery paths. By the time it’s ready to start working, a meaningful chunk of the context window is already used.
The more robust I made the startup instructions, the less room the agent had left to do the actual work.
The coordination layer and the execution layer are competing for the same finite resource. That’s not a tuning problem. It’s a ceiling.
I had built a distributed system where the only work state lived in volatile memory.
Bigger context windows move the ceiling, but they don’t remove it — because the problem is where state lives.
And crash recovery is just the failure that made the ceiling visible. Once I started looking, I found it everywhere.
Drift. An agent that boots fresh is sharp — it follows the architecture, respects the conventions, implements what the spec asks for. But agents don’t stay fresh. As the context window fills with tool calls, file reads, error traces, and corrections, adherence to the original spec erodes. Gradually. The agent hardcodes something that should be configurable. It reimplements a utility function that already exists because it didn’t search far enough. It drifts from the architecture in ways that are individually minor and collectively corrosive. An agent that was sharp at message 10 is unreliable by message 80. Not because the model changed — because the accumulated context diluted the signal of the original spec.
Attention fragmentation. At four agents, I could keep rough track of each one. At ten, I couldn’t. The agents I checked on made progress. The agents I forgot about drifted, stalled, or went off-spec — and I didn’t find out until I manually inspected their output. My attention became the allocation mechanism, and like any scarce resource allocated without a system, it went to whatever was loudest, not whatever was most important.
Invisible progress. The inverse of drift: an agent finishes its task and sits idle, burning credits, waiting for instructions I forgot to give. At any given moment, I couldn’t answer “what’s done?” without polling every terminal window. There was no dashboard, no status layer, no way to see the state of work without interrupting my own focus to check.
Merge conflicts. Agent A finishes refactoring a module and commits. Agent B, which started before that commit, is still working against the old version. Agent C reads Agent A’s commit but misinterprets the intent because the commit message doesn’t capture the architectural reasoning. None of them know what the others are doing in real time. Git provides eventual coordination, but eventual isn’t fast enough when four agents are making concurrent changes to interconnected modules. Every session with parallel agents produced at least one merge conflict. I’d come back from a fifteen-minute break and spend thirty minutes reconciling what the agents had done independently.
Each of these failures is fixable in isolation. You can restart drifted agents. You can check tabs more often. You can write better commit messages. But fixing them one at a time is the wrong frame. The failures aren’t independent — they’re symptoms of the same structural limitation: a coordination model that routes everything through a human’s working memory and a finite context window.
This is the same pathology I encountered in my quantitative research. I wrote about it in my PPO-to-SAC migration post: I was evaluating my RL agent on global Sharpe ratio and it looked fine. The agent appeared to be learning. But when I decomposed performance by market regime — sliced the results by the conditions that actually mattered — the aggregate hid a mess. The agent was doing well in the dominant regime and quietly failing everywhere else.
Multi-agent development at Stage 7 has the same shape. The aggregate output looks productive. Commits are landing. Features are taking shape. But the per-agent, per-task decomposition reveals coordination failures that no amount of better models or longer context windows will fix. The problem isn’t model intelligence. It’s the absence of a coordination architecture that can persist state, distribute work, and resolve conflicts independently of a human’s attention span.
That’s the wall. And on the other side of it is a different kind of system entirely.
What Stage 8 Looks Like in Practice
After hitting the wall on Quanto, I started looking at how other people were solving the same problem. Most weren’t — most developers at Stage 6 or 7 were either living with the chaos or retreating to fewer agents. But a few were building orchestration layers. The most ambitious of these is Gas Town, Steve Yegge’s multi-agent orchestration system for Claude Code.
I’m not going to walk through Gas Town’s full architecture — Yegge’s posts do that, and they’re worth reading. What I want to focus on is the three coordination patterns that distinguish orchestrated multi-agent from hand-managed multi-agent, because these patterns are what the wall demands, regardless of which tool implements them.
Persistent state outside agent memory. This is the pattern that addresses the catch-22 directly. In Gas Town, work units — called Beads — live in Git-backed storage, not in any agent’s context window. If an agent crashes forty-seven minutes into a pipeline, the work state survives. Another agent can pick up the bead, read the state, and resume. The context window is no longer the single point of failure for work-in-progress.
This is the same principle that made SAC viable for my quantitative research when PPO failed. PPO is on-policy: it uses each data point for a few gradient steps and throws it away. When your training data is finite — which in financial markets, it always is — that’s fatal. SAC stores every transition in a replay buffer and samples from it repeatedly. The valuable state persists outside the learning cycle. Gas Town’s Beads solve the same structural problem at a different scale: valuable work state persists outside the agent’s session. The medium changes. The principle doesn’t.
Delegated routing. In my document-based system, I was the router. Every task assignment, every priority call, every “what should this agent work on next” decision flowed through me. That’s the attention fragmentation problem — my attention was the allocation mechanism, and it didn’t scale. In an orchestrated system, a coordinator decomposes intent into tasks and assigns them. You describe what you want built. The system figures out who works on what. You’re operating at the intent level, not the terminal level.
Automated verification and merge resolution. Agents that check other agents’ work. A merge queue that handles conflicts between concurrent changes. This is what the “agents can’t see each other” problem demands: a layer that sits above the individual agents and maintains coherence across parallel streams of work. Not perfect coherence — but enough to catch the conflicts before they compound.
These patterns aren’t unique to Gas Town. They’re what any serious orchestration layer will need to implement, because they’re direct responses to the structural failures of Stage 7. Persistent state solves crash recovery — and makes drift recoverable by letting you rehydrate a fresh agent without losing the work. Delegated routing solves attention fragmentation. Automated verification solves merge conflicts and invisible progress. The specific implementation will vary. The coordination patterns won’t.
What a session feels like with this kind of system: you describe what you want built. Work fans out across multiple agents. Progress is tracked externally — in durable storage, not in your head. You check status, not terminals. The shift is from managing execution to managing intent.
The Honest Tradeoffs
I want to be careful here, because the narrative arc of this article — from friction to framework to solution — makes it tempting to end on a clean resolution. But the reality is messier than that.
The cost is real. Multi-agent orchestration at this level burns serious money. Multiple Claude Pro Max subscriptions, aggressive API usage. Yegge has been transparent about this — he’s talked openly about maxing out weekly limits across multiple plans. This isn’t a weekend project. It’s an infrastructure investment, and for most developers, the ROI isn’t there yet.
The maturity is early. Gas Town is months old. The chaos is part of the experience — session limits, agents that go off the rails, maintenance processes that need babysitting of their own. Yegge is building in public, which means the rough edges are visible. That’s honest, but it also means anyone adopting this today is adopting frontier-stage tooling with frontier-stage instability.
Most developers don’t need this. If you’re productive at Stage 5 or 6, the complexity overhead of orchestration isn’t worth it. The coordination wall is real, but it only hits you when you’re operating at a scale where parallelism matters — large codebases, complex interdependent systems, tight timelines. A solo developer building a SaaS product with one focused agent is probably better served by that one agent than by an orchestration layer. Start vanilla. Add complexity when you hit actual limits, not theoretical ones.
The value of Stage 8 is diagnostic, not prescriptive. This is the point I want to be most precise about. You don’t need to run thirty parallel agents to benefit from understanding why persistent state, delegated routing, and automated verification matter. Understanding the coordination patterns gives you a framework for recognizing when your current stage is structurally breaking — the same way understanding SAC’s principles improved how I evaluated PPO, even when PPO was still the right choice for simpler environments. The patterns are a diagnostic lens. The tools are optional.
The half-life applies here too. Gas Town is built on 2025-era Claude Code. When the underlying agents evolve — and they will — the orchestration layer will need to evolve with them. You’re not escaping the treadmill by moving to Stage 8. You’re running it at a higher altitude. The paradigm compression that brought us from Stage 1 to Stage 8 in two years will compress the stages above Stage 8 just as fast.
Regime Detection
The obvious takeaway from this article is “learn orchestration” or “get to Stage 8.” That’s not the takeaway — the same way my PPO-to-SAC post wasn’t about SAC.
In that migration, the breakthrough wasn’t switching algorithms. It was building a regime-conditional evaluation framework that decomposed aggregate performance into something I could actually learn from. Global Sharpe looked fine. Regime-conditional Sharpe told a different story. The skill wasn’t willingness to try a new algorithm. It was building the lens that made the old algorithm’s structural failure visible.
The same pattern applies here. The skill that matters isn’t learning Gas Town, or Claude Code, or whatever orchestration framework comes next. It’s the ability to recognize when your current paradigm is structurally degrading — and to articulate why.
Adaptability is learning the new tool once the shift is obvious. Regime detection is recognizing structural degradation before the shift is obvious — and being able to name the assumption that broke.
There are signs. They’re recognizable once you know to look for them. You’re spending more time managing the tool than using it. Your productivity has plateaued despite the tool getting better. The problems you’re solving are coordination problems, not capability problems. You keep wishing for “one more feature” that’s actually a different paradigm. Each of these is a diagnostic signal, not a complaint.
Yes, this article’s specific recommendations have a half-life too. The 8 stages will be outdated by 2027. Gas Town may be replaced by something unrecognizable. But the framework — recognizing when coordination costs exceed capability gains, treating plateaus as diagnostic signals rather than skill deficits — that transfers. It transferred from quantitative research to AI-assisted development for me. It’ll transfer to whatever comes next.
That afternoon with six terminals and the canonicalization module — that wasn’t a productivity failure. It was a diagnostic signal. The paradigm was breaking. I just didn’t have the framework to see it yet.
The real skill isn’t orchestrating agents. It’s shortening the gap between when a paradigm starts failing and when you notice.
The platform I built for my quantitative research — data ingestion, feature engineering, deterministic experiment management, regime-aware evaluation — is open source: Quanto on GitHub. I wrote more about the reproducibility architecture and the PPO-to-SAC migration on Medium.
